source_texts: “What? A priest? It’s not necessary. . . . Where would we get the money to pay him? I have no sins! Even without that God should forgive me. . . . He knows how I’ve suffered! And if He doesn’t forgive me, so be it!”
‘How can you doubt it! But you, who do not love her, why are you so ready to endanger your life? It must be that you hate Erik.’

He told them. But we shall not repeat it here. He told the story briefly and impersonally. He did not speak of his love and his feelings; he told them only that he was no longer so determined to shoot himself “in view of certain new developments.” He did not bother to explain the motives behind his actions anymore, did not go into subtleties. Besides, his interrogators did not interrupt him very often to ask him questions; it was obvious that this was not what interested them most.
The ‘principal tenant’ of Jean Valjean’s day had died and had been replaced by another exactly like her. I am not sure which philosopher said, ‘There is never any shortage of old women.’
yells Mikolka, the crowbar still in his hands, his eyes bloodshot. He stands there as if feeling sorry that there’s nothing more to strike.

“I mean those clubs, those Dussot’s, and those spits of land,§ perhaps, that’s progress for you—well, that’ll all be without me,” he continued, once more taking no note of the question. “Besides, who wants to be a cardsharp?”

The officer’s comrades noticed in that ‘neglected’ garden, behind that sorry-looking rococo gate, quite a pretty creature who was almost always there when the handsome lieutenant went by. This lieutenant is not unknown to the reader: his name was Théodule Gillenormand.

The horses had turned to the right, and they started on their preliminary canter, passing in front of the grand stand in a disordered crowd. Then the excited remarks broke out again; every one spoke at the same time.
“Thandavaraya, I don’t think this is a real horse. It may belong to the poi kaal kudirai (false horse performance) group,” said one of the soldiers. “Fine then.
“Yes,” said Rose Mignon, “his foot caught in a trap-door and he has sprained his ankle most abominably. If you had only heard him swear, with his leg all tied up and stretched out on a chair! ”
rationales: Outputs 1 and 3 share the same paraphraser fingerprint: both use clean, ellipsis-free sentences, both end with the phrase 'so be it,' both omit or minimally handle the 'spare rouble' detail, and both restructure the text into concise complete sentences rather than fragmented speech. Their stylistic approach, level of paraphrasing, and word choices (particularly 'so be it' and the omission of the ellipsis-heavy style) strongly suggest they came from the same system.

Outputs 1 and 3 both reformulate the text as pointed rhetorical questions, add interpretive expansions beyond the source (e.g., 'You must despise Erik' and 'personal vendetta against Erik'), and use similar accusatory framing. Both use double quotes and 'Erik' spelling, and both shift toward a question-based interrogative structure that goes beyond the literal source text.

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical linguistic fingerprints: both open with 'He shared the information with them,' both describe the account as 'concise and detached,' both use 'less inclined' regarding suicide, both use 'Furthermore' as a transitional word, and both conclude with 'suggesting their primary interest lay elsewhere.' These structural and lexical parallels strongly indicate they were produced by the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 4 share nearly identical sentence structure: '[title] of [Jean Valjean's day/the time of Jean Valjean] was dead, and had been replaced by another exactly like her.' Both place the philosopher quote after the landlady statement, use a formal/archaic register for the philosopher attribution ('I know not' vs 'I do not remember'), and render the quote in very similar ways ('Old women are never lacking' vs 'There is never any lack of old women'). These structural and stylistic fingerprints strongly suggest they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 both use present tense narration ('cries'/'shouts', 'stands'/'lingers'), share a similar dramatic rhythm, and follow the same structural sequence: action with crowbar → bloodshot eyes → lingering → regretting nothing left to hit. All other outputs use past tense and have distinctly different stylistic fingerprints.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same distinctive sentence structure: the main quoted speech, followed by 'he went on, again [ignoring/without noticing] the question,' and then a closing rhetorical question about being a card shark/cheat. This 'he went on, again... the question' construction is a unique fingerprint not found in the other outputs. Both also maintain the same clause order and dialogue formatting style, strongly suggesting they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the same distinctive two-sentence structure beginning with 'This lieutenant is [not] unknown to the reader. His name was Théodule Gillenormand.' Both then follow with 'The comrades of the officer/this officer had noticed in this [neglected] garden, behind this [miserable/pathetic] rococo gate, a [very] pretty creature who almost always [appeared/was there] when the handsome lieutenant passed.' The sentence segmentation, ordering of information, and core vocabulary choices (neglected garden, comrades, pretty creature, almost always, handsome lieutenant) are nearly identical, pointing to the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical structure: both begin with the crowd reaction ('excited comments/remarks broke out again'), followed by 'everyone spoke at once/at the same time,' then describe the horses turning right and performing a 'pre-race/preliminary canter,' passing in front of the 'grandstands' in a 'disorderly crowd.' The sentence ordering, word choices, and structural flow are extremely similar, suggesting they came from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use third-person narrative prose (no direct dialogue), both include the Tamil term 'poikaal kudirai' with an inline explanatory gloss, and both describe the soldier's suggestion in an indirect/reported manner rather than dramatizing it through dialogue. Their sentence structure and approach to conveying the scene are distinctly parallel compared to the other outputs.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the same stylistic pattern: both use the conditional 'If [only] you [could/had only] hear(d) him swearing' construction, both describe the leg as 'tied' to/up on a chair, both use 'trap door' (two words), and both employ emphatic language for the sprain ('most abominably' / 'fearful sprain'). These linguistic fingerprints strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.
